data.title: What if Powell chooses to stay and continue the hardline stance?
data.description:
01

Powell is stepping down as chairman in May, so why launch a criminal investigation at this time?

Because he was disobedient, because he was too slow, because he didn't cut interest rates fast enough, and because if he remained on the board, Trump would lose an opportunity to appoint his own people.

But it may not be entirely about him, but rather setting a rule for everyone—other members of the Federal Reserve Board, and officials throughout the federal government—to see what happens if they don't obey.

But it may not be entirely about him, but rather setting a rule for everyone—other members of the Federal Reserve Board, and officials throughout the federal government—to see what happens if they don't obey.

In any case, this is no longer just a matter of central bank independence, nor is it something as mild and innocuous as interfering in interest rate decisions. It has become an act of threatening "political opponents." The political term "terror" (French: terreur), originating from the Reign of Terror during the French Revolution, refers to the persecution of dissidents. Such behavior is not uncommon in politics, but its use against a central bank governor is truly unprecedented in American history. However, I myself was somewhat surprised by Mr. Powell's response. Throughout his term, Powell deliberately avoided political issues and did not respond to pressure from Trump. He could have easily dodged the issue this time, but he didn't; instead, he issued a direct video statement saying, "This is because we didn't set interest rates according to the president's wishes." Completely unvarnished and very direct. His words are often firm as he prepares to leave. This makes one somewhat more inclined to admire him. So, the question arises: Powell's term as chairman expires in May, but his term as a governor doesn't end until January 2028. Conventionally, a chairman usually leaves the council after leaving office. However, he can choose not to leave. What if he chooses to stay and continue his hardline stance? Polymarket immediately picked up on this possibility—before the announcement, the probability of Powell leaving the Federal Reserve Board by the end of the year was around 85%; after the announcement, it immediately dropped to 75%. This indicates that the market is already reassessing—the court subpoena was the final straw for Powell, and it's possible he'll stick to his guns and hold onto his board seat until 2028. This has historical precedent. In 1948, after Truman refused to allow Marriner Eccles to run for re-election as president, she chose to remain on the board for another three years, until 1951. Eccles later played a key role in facilitating the 1951 Federal Reserve-Treasury Agreement, which established the Federal Reserve's independence.

Many major historical turning points often involve unexpected figures who "acted cowardly their whole lives," only to suddenly undergo a complete personality change at the end, thus propelling the course of history.

02

The feud between US presidents and Federal Reserve chairmen is actually a tradition: for example, Lyndon Johnson vs. William Martin, Nixon vs. Arthur Burns, and Volcker vs. almost the entire American society.

However, the use of the Department of Justice for a criminal investigation is unprecedented in the 112-year history of the Federal Reserve.

Due to its shocking nature, the event prompted central bank governors from around the world to issue a joint letter of solidarity in support of Powell and protest against Trump's political attacks. It evoked a strong sense of justice.

This event is a historic moment in the beacon of the United States, but not in other "non-beacon" countries around the world. In 2023, Polish President Donald Tusk came to power, and one of his campaign promises was to remove Adam Glapiński, the governor of the National Bank of Poland, accusing him of politicizing monetary policy and favoring the previous government. Immediately after being elected, less than a year later, the Polish government subpoenaed Grapinski, accusing him of engaging in illegal public finance operations, constituting a crime. Grapinski's response was not to release a video, but to appeal to the European Central Bank (ECB). ECB President Lagarde immediately wrote a formal letter in support of Grapinski, warning the Polish authorities against taking illegal actions. Here's the key point: In this incident, the global central bank circle, at least the European Central Bank circle, did not stand on the side of the "victim," nor did it collectively voice its support as it did for Powell. The matter was left unresolved. This is interesting: Is the "independence" card something that varies from person to person (or country to country)? I'm not an expert on Eastern European issues, so there are certainly many details I haven't covered. But this contrast itself is worth pondering. Central bank governors are accustomed to viewing themselves as technocrats, transcending partisan politics, neither left nor right, and only recognizing data. I wonder if you've ever felt this way (I have)—whenever I hear the word "independence," I can't help but feel a sense of awe, as if this basic requirement places them on a moral high ground. But this self-perception overlooks a growing change: technocratic governance itself, in many people's eyes, is no longer a neutral and objective entity—because "neutrality" itself is also a political stance—at least from the perspective of the so-called populist wave, "technocrats" may precisely represent what they are rebelling against: a group of elites making decisions for the people behind the scenes. In this context, the defense of technocratic governance and institutional independence is no longer neutral. You may think you're above politics, but to others, you are part of it. What will happen next? My first thought was—the Fed Chair AI prediction competition is becoming more suspenseful and unpredictable (the competition introduction is here: "Two Kevins Run Fast: Human Intuition vs. AI Intelligence Analysis vs. Market Collective Wisdom"; the progress is here: "Fed Chair Prediction Competition Update 1: Can AI Find the 'Behind-the-Scenes Manipulator'?")—and that's exactly what I wanted—variables, new situations, and new narratives are the best opportunities to demonstrate the value of AI in "discovering narratives." The selection of the next Federal Reserve Chairman has thus become more uncertain. The probability of Powell deciding to remain on the board and stand up to Trump is already a new disruptive factor, and changes in the board will also affect the final nomination. Meanwhile, external influencing factors are also beginning to change. A Republican senator (North Carolina Senator Thom Tillis) has already issued a statement indicating that he will oppose any Trump-nominated Fed candidate, including Powell's successor, until the legal issues are resolved. A second possible impact is that being a civil servant in the United States has become a high-risk profession. Truly capable and insightful people might consider entering politics again and again. The logic is very simple, and you can probably guess: If I offend Trump and get a summons—I'll have to spend a fortune on lawyers and waste time and energy on litigation. I might never be able to work normally again. This is the "chilling effect": when the government uses all its power against an individual, "retaliating" becomes an extreme luxury. Third, and perhaps most importantly: what about the market? When the event first occurred, the market reaction was quite mild, and long-term US Treasury yields did not plummet. Did we overreact, and that capital wasn't worried? Not necessarily. If we were to give this event a name, "institutional rupture" wouldn't be an exaggeration. The process of repricing the global financial credit system is somewhat like bankruptcy—a slow collapse, then a sudden crash. As long as the Federal Reserve Board retains some restraining power, the market reaction may not be too significant; but once a certain critical point is reached, change can happen "overnight, in an instant." Thinking further ahead: the very foundation of the US dollar could be weakened as a result. It's well known that the Federal Reserve's power extends beyond setting interest rates; it also controls the financial system's water, electricity, and networks (the dollar payment system and financial infrastructure). However, this instability in the foundation won't be reflected in Treasury yields.

data.title: Cryptocurrency will become a daily payment tool for Russian residents amid Western containment.
data.description:
As the West tightens its financial blockade against Russia, cryptocurrency, originally seen as a "decentralized experiment," has been thrust into the core of Russia's countermeasures. It wasn't "chosen," but rather forced to take center stage as traditional channels became increasingly ineffective. From redefining it at the legislative level to the actual entry of banks and exchanges, and the coordinated implementation of taxation, mining, and cross-border settlement systems, Russia is transforming cryptocurrency from a passively tolerated, marginal option into a financial tool that can be institutionally controlled. For Russia, sanctions are no longer just pressure, but a trigger to formally integrate cryptocurrency into the financial system.

 Encryption is no longer "special"

If we turn back the clock three years, Russia's attitude towards cryptocurrencies was still quite cautious.

In 2021, Russian President Vladimir Putin publicly acknowledged the value of cryptocurrencies but explicitly denied their applicability to oil trade.

In 2022, the Russian Ministry of Finance further prohibited the acceptance of Bitcoin for oil exports.

However, this stance was gradually overturned by reality over the following three years.

In March 2025, according to informed sources, Russia had begun using cryptocurrencies as a settlement tool in its oil trade with India to circumvent Western sanctions.

On January 14, 2026, this change was formally incorporated into policy. The Chairman of the Financial Market Committee of the Russian State Duma publicly confirmed that a bill to remove cryptocurrencies from the "special financial supervision" framework has been prepared. This may promote the widespread adoption of cryptocurrencies in the lives of Russian residents in the future. According to the proposed plan: Ordinary residents can participate in cryptocurrency asset allocation (with a set amount limit) Professional institutions will no longer be restricted Cryptocurrencies will be allowed for international settlement Related assets can enter other countries' markets after being issued in Russia This is not a "loosening of regulations," but rather a redefinition of the position of cryptocurrencies in the financial system.

  

Over the past year, Russia has faced a set of highly realistic constraints:

  • Russian legal entities and individuals continue to face restrictions on using traditional currencies for overseas payments
  • Decreased efficiency and unstable pathways in foreign trade settlements
  • Financial institutions have been forced to seek alternative settlement methods

Against this backdrop, the Central Bank of Russia has acknowledged in official statements that:

Relaxing cryptocurrency circulation rules is a necessary choice under these realistic constraints.

data.title: Where are the profit opportunities in the crypto market in 2026?
data.description: This is a list of ten core predictions for the crypto market in 2026, covering important topics such as Bitcoin price outlook, mainstream altcoin narratives, and the prospects for the integration of crypto and artificial intelligence. The content is presented by renowned analyst Miles Deutscher and is part of his annual strategy analysis, used to effectively clarify the direction of investment in the new year. Even if you don't completely agree with all the views in this article, it is hoped that it will inspire you to think, help you build your own predictive framework, and prepare you to seize the biggest upward opportunities of the year. 10. Predicting a 5x Increase in Market Trading Volume Since January/February 2025, predicted market trading volume has increased approximately tenfold. Miles believes this trend will continue, predicting that by 2026, at least one month's trading volume will reach five times the current level. Based on current figures, a 5x increase would translate to approximately $95 billion in monthly trading volume. Another key driver is the rise of derivative protocols built around prediction markets (the first leveraged prediction market is under development), which will further amplify trading volume. 9. Explosive Growth in Perpetual Contract Trading Miles' core logic is: Perpetual contracts will continue to penetrate mainstream asset classes, no longer limited to crypto token trading. By 2025, perpetual contract technology based on crypto infrastructure will achieve breakthroughs in instant settlement, user experience, and decentralization. Secondly, gold, metals, and stocks are in a strong bull market cycle. Historical experience shows that when market cycles near their peak, investors tend to move further down the risk curve in pursuit of returns (perpetual contracts will benefit along with small-cap stocks/emerging markets). Furthermore, in many scenarios, accessing these assets through crypto channels is far more convenient and efficient than through the traditional financial system. Based on these multiple factors, Miles predicts an explosive growth in stock/metal trading volume on decentralized perpetual contract exchanges, with perpetual contract trading categories far exceeding the scope of crypto tokens. 8. The ICO Revival Continues Significant changes in the regulatory environment have unprecedentedly broadened the channels for public participation in token sales. Current ICO participation is at an all-time high, and Miles expects this upward trend to continue. 7. Altcoins Favor Stronger Profit Models The market trend has clearly shifted towards favoring projects with real business potential, rather than purely speculative targets. Of course, speculation will always exist, but more and more investors (regardless of size) are searching for the intrinsic value that truly supports token value amidst the dilution of abundant liquidity. Miles believes that protocols with real profitability will continue to dominate. In short, protocols with actual revenue mechanisms will be more sustainable than projects without substantial returns. Focus on companies/projects/teams that truly generate revenue. 6. RWA to Experience Second-Best Year in History Miles believes RWA (Real-World Assets) will have its best year ever, but technically, surpassing the $14 billion growth target of 2025 will be challenging. Liquidity is expected to continue flowing into the sector, maintaining high growth, but the percentage growth rate may be lower than in 2025. A large number of yield generation/asset tokenization opportunities will emerge in 2026, offering a wide range of altcoin trading options. 5. Digital Banking Sector Sees Fastest Growth Crypto asset/stablecoin banking seems to have finally reached a turning point. Infrastructure has gradually matched market demand, driving explosive growth in many services. The ability to easily convert between crypto assets and fiat currency is a solution urgently needed by the market. This sector boasts the largest reachable market (almost the entire financial world) and is actively addressing many pain points in developing countries and regions underserved by traditional finance. This is a massive market, and Miles anticipates a true breakthrough in 2026. 4. AI and AI Agents Return to Their Essence Last January, the frenzied AI hype drove up the price of crypto-AI fusion protocols, but the technology was not yet mature. Entering 2026, the situation is different; the technology finally meets market expectations. Miles believes that 2026 will undoubtedly be a pivotal year for AI development (although it is every year), and retail investors' speculative enthusiasm for AI's potential could easily spill over into the crypto space. The convergence of crypto and AI has a perfect synergy: Crypto provides freedom in financial channels; AI brings automation capabilities, representing the future of finance. This is expected to spawn opportunities in multiple AI sub-sectors, including x402, bots, agent workflows, and AI data/infrastructure. 3. Stablecoin Supply Grows Over 50% Last year, the total supply of stablecoins increased by 50% (from $200 billion to $300 billion). Catalyzed by the US GENIUS Act (a stablecoin regulatory act), Miles expects a similar increase this year. 2. Institutions Will Become the Dominant Force in the Crypto Market This cycle has been driven by institutional forces (such as digital asset trusts and ETF development). Miles expects this institutionally dominated market structure to continue to deepen. Investors should focus on tokens/protocols that can attract institutional interest (returning to the core view of focusing on projects with actual profitability). 1. Bitcoin's year-end closing price will be higher than the beginning of the year. Whether Bitcoin will experience a crazy surge to over $150,000 this year depends on multiple factors: Capital inflows/buyer demand; Digital asset trusts; Macroeconomic background. Miles believes that while it's impossible to predict whether all positive factors will trigger extreme market movements, he firmly believes that Bitcoin will close with a positive annual candle in 2026. This means BTC needs to close above the $90,000 range. Miles' bullish logic for predicting a year-end rally is based on the following: We are in the final year of the business cycle. The current price bottoming pattern is similar to previous years.

data.title: In-depth analysis of the technology access requirements of the Senate draft of the Clarity Act
data.description:
Executive Summary

On January 15, 2026, the U.S. Senate Banking Committee is scheduled to hold an executive meeting in Room 538 of Dirksen to formally "mark" the revised text of H.R.3633 - The Clarity Act of 2025. This act marks a fundamental shift in U.S. regulatory logic: from a "qualitative era" relying on administrative discretion to a "quantitative era" based on the underlying architecture of blockchain. This report will provide an in-depth analysis of the specific constraints imposed by the Act on Virtual Asset Service Providers (VASPs) in three core areas: Know Your Asset (KYA), Stablecoin Reserve Transparency, and Technical Compliance with the Bank Secrecy Act, and will assess its profound impact on industry infrastructure. I. Fact Check on Legal Process and Institutional Evolution According to Congressional legislative tracking records and the latest dynamics of the political game as of January 14, 2026, the legislative path of H.R.3633 is as follows: House of Representatives Stage: On July 17, 2025, the House of Representatives passed the original draft with a vote of 294 to 134, establishing the initial framework for the division of functions between the SEC and CFTC. Senate Game: After being transferred to the Senate on September 18, 2025, the bill stalled due to concerns about the impact of stablecoins on traditional bank reserves. Revision Consensus: On January 12, 2026, Senate Banking Committee Chairman Tim Scott released the latest revised text after bipartisan negotiations. Key Milestone: Tomorrow (January 15th) at 10:00 AM, the committee's "mark" vote will determine whether the amendment is legally eligible for a full Senate vote. Institutional Observation: The Senate's revised version, compared to the House's original version, further strengthens the criminal liability constraints on issuers of "permissioned payment stablecoins" and refines the verification procedures for "blockchain maturity." II. KYA's Technical Hard Constraints: Section 205 "Mature Blockchain" Criterion Sec. 205 of the bill is the "logical anchor" of the entire bill, replacing the highly controversial Howey Test with computable metrics.

1. Penetration Analysis of the 20% Control Red Line

The draft amendment proposes that for a system to obtain "digital goods" designation under the jurisdiction of the CFTC, it must prove that it has met the following power distribution indicators in the past 12 months:

  • Government Concentration:The total "voting power" held by the issuer, affiliates, and parties acting in concert shall not exceed 20%...

    ...

    .

    • Technical Benchmarking: TrustIn's KYA Pro (Institutional-Grade Deep Due Diligence) module provides configurable 3-5-9 layer multi-level penetration analysis. Through its "white-box" engine, compliance teams can penetrate surface addresses and deeply explore the entity connection paths behind different addresses. This helps VASPs accurately assess the actual governance concentration of assets to be listed, determine whether it triggers the red line in the law regarding "issuers and related parties holding no more than 20% of shares," and thus provide conclusive evidence of entity connections for CFTC maturity certification. 2. Dynamic Monitoring of Reserve Addresses in Sec. 512 The bill requires stablecoin issuers to conduct monthly "Examination" audits and imposes criminal liability on executives, which forces VASPs to maintain real-time vigilance regarding the underlying risks of the stablecoins they connect to.

      • Technical Benchmarking: TrustIn's Monitoring module provides 24/7 dynamic defense. Utilizing its "anti-pollution" mechanism, VASP can continuously scan core reserve addresses related to stablecoins for risk. Once interaction between a reserve address and a sanctioned entity is detected, the system will issue a risk warning in real time and isolate the risk. This not only fills the "time window" of monthly audit reports but also provides VASP with a clear audit trail through "white-box" recording, meeting the regulatory requirements for rigorous compliance records. 3. Complex Link Reconstruction for Sec. 110 (TraceForce) The Act mandates the use of “appropriate analytics tools” to monitor Suspicious Activity (SAR), especially in environments with active P2P and self-custodial wallets, where the accuracy of link tracing is crucial. Technology Comparison: In response to the Act’s legal requirement for “distributed ledger analytics capabilities,” TrustIn’s TraceForce (law enforcement-grade fund tracing) module demonstrates its core competitiveness. This module focuses on penetrating mixing protocols and cross-chain technology, enabling the visualization and reconstruction of complex fund flows, particularly filling data intelligence blind spots in Asia and the Middle East. Through TraceForce's case snapshots and team collaboration features, VASP can efficiently collect evidence on P2P deposits protected under Sec. 105(c), ensuring that while identifying money laundering risks, it provides evidence of suspicious activity that meets federal enforcement standards. Conclusion H.R.3633 is not the final draft of digital asset regulation, but it has established a clear technological theme for 2026: decentralized quantitative monitoring, penetrating audits of stablecoins, and the legal status of on-chain analytics tools. **Report Disclaimer:** This research report is based on the draft legislation and proposed amendments published before January 14, 2026. As the bill is still under review, the final wording may be slightly adjusted as the voting process progresses. The technical applications mentioned in this report are for VASP compliance reference only and do not constitute official endorsement of cooperation or legal advice.

data.title: The more it's banned, the higher the price goes? Unveiling the truth behind the XMR price surge.
data.description:
Author: Frank, PANews

XMR (Monero), one of the leading players in the privacy coin market, hit a new all-time high on January 13th, with its spot price exceeding $690, reigniting market discussions about privacy coins.

From January 2025 to the present, XMR has surged from around $200, a maximum increase of 262%. This surge is extremely rare given the current weakness of mainstream altcoins. Even more intriguing is that this rally occurred against the backdrop of unprecedented global regulatory tightening.

Due to compliance pressures, major centralized exchanges such as Binance have already delisted XMR for spot trading. On January 12th, the Dubai Virtual Assets Regulatory Authority (VARA) officially announced a ban on the trading and custody of privacy tokens throughout Dubai and its free zones.

However, this ban not only failed to cast a shadow over XMR, but instead pushed it to new highs, providing a mockery of the Dubai government. Amidst the liquidity crunch on exchanges and the wielding of regulatory power, who is the real driving force behind XMR's surge? PANews peels back the surface to find the true demand behind this rally. Exchanges are not the core pricing venues. Despite the market's fervor, it's not driven by funds within exchanges. In the spot market, while XMR's recent trading volume has increased with the price rise, it has remained largely within the range of tens of millions to $200 million, without any particularly dramatic increase. Historically, the truly significant peak in spot trading volume was reached on November 10th at $410 million. This means that spot trading (or spot buying on centralized exchanges) was not the biggest driver of the recent doubling of XMR prices. The situation is similar with contracts. The peak in trading volume also occurred on November 10th. Subsequently, until about a week ago, contract trading volume did not show a significant increase, and even showed some signs of decline. Observing the open interest data, its USD-denominated change curve almost completely overlaps with the price trend. There has been no abnormal surge in the number of XMR positions in the market; the increase in open interest is solely due to the price increase, rather than a large influx of new funds. Clearly, mainstream exchanges are not the core venue for current XMR pricing. Supply-Side Undercurrents: Miner Reshuffling and Early Positioning

Since the "overt" funding is unremarkable, we need to turn to the "under-the-table" on-chain world. XMR, as the most privacy-focused network, has very little information to mine, but changes in mining difficulty and mining rewards can give us insight into capital's positioning on the supply side.

Historical mining difficulty usually represents the enthusiasm of capital participating in the network ecosystem. Data shows that XMR's mining difficulty began to climb rapidly at the end of 2024, and remained in a state of rapid growth throughout the first half of 2025. Although there were some fluctuations between September and November, a new round of difficulty increases has recently begun.

Here's an interesting anecdote: In September, the Qubic project claimed to control over 51% of the XMR network's hashrate and conducted a "demonstration attack," causing a chain reorganization of the XMR network for 18 blocks. This incident served as a wake-up call for the community, and subsequently, many miners migrated their hashrate to the established mining pool SupportXMR. This incident was the main reason for the dramatic fluctuations in mining difficulty at the end of 2025, but it also indirectly confirmed the activity and resilience of the hashrate market.

More noteworthy is the correlation between the mining reward curve and difficulty.

Prior to April 2025, Monero network mining revenue experienced a significant drop. Looking at the difficulty graph at that time, the hashrate surged, but the price remained volatile. This divergence diluted revenue, potentially forcing some smaller, higher-cost miners out of the market. Data shows that mining difficulty briefly dipped in April, confirming this hypothesis. This was a classic example of "miner capitulation" and "coin exchange." Afterwards, with a sharp price increase, mining revenue and difficulty once again rose in tandem. Looking at the data changes during this period, it's possible that as early as the beginning of 2025, some large mining companies or capital firms with strong risk resistance may have already begun to invest in Monero token mining despite low returns. Demand-side validation: Paying a high premium for privacy. If miners represent confidence on the supply side, then average transaction fees most accurately reflect user demand. As the chart shows, Monero's average transaction fees remained relatively stable before the first half of 2025, generally below $0.1. However, a growth trend began to emerge in June; by December 11th, the highest average fee reached over $0.3, more than three times higher than six months prior.

Because Monero has a dynamic block scaling mechanism, the surge in fees means that a large number of users are trying to send transactions quickly and are willing to pay high fees to compensate miners for the scaling costs. This indirectly proves that from the second half of 2025, the demand for real transactions on the Monero chain began to increase significantly.

However, we also found an interesting pattern: the surge in on-chain fees often coincides with a price surge.

For example, on April 28th, XMR suddenly surged by 14%, with the average transaction fee soaring to $0.125 that day; however, during the subsequent slow price climb, the fee plummeted back to its lowest point ($0.058 on May 4th). This illustrates that while price fluctuations can temporarily drive on-chain demand, demand also calms down once the volatility subsides. Although sometimes the two don't move in tandem (e.g., fees rose on May 14th but the price didn't), overall, over the past six months, price increases have temporarily boosted on-chain demand, and the actual increase in on-chain demand, in turn, has fueled market optimism for XMR; the two are mutually reinforcing. The Truth Behind Both Sides Based on the above data, the surge in XMR may have a "two-sided" truth. The so-called "white" refers to the "anti-fragile" rebound of privacy demands under regulatory pressure. The reverse driving force of regulation is becoming increasingly apparent. Dubai's VARA ban not only failed to destroy XMR, but also made market participants realize that regulators can ban exchanges, but not the protocol itself. When major exchanges withdrew from XMR trading, the logic of pricing based on market makers and derivative contracts was rewritten, and XMR returned to a model controlled by real users or certain heavyweight players. After breaking away from the exchange system, privacy coins have developed an independent rhythm completely different from the mainstream market. The so-called "black" refers to the capital game under information asymmetry. Behind this opacity, there may be hidden "market manipulators." The lackluster trading data (even on January 13th, when prices hit a new high, open interest was only $240 million, and liquidations amounted to just over $1 million) indicates that mainstream institutions were largely unable to predict and participate in this surge, only able to follow the trend. This information asymmetry, controlled by a minority, leads to extreme price volatility. Especially when the market begins to focus on such a surge, it often foreshadows short-term overheating. Referencing the privacy coin ZEC in November, it experienced a pullback of over 50% after its surge. Ultimately, the privacy coin market suffers from significant information asymmetry, placing ordinary retail investors at a significant disadvantage. Amidst the volatile price movements of privacy coins, on-chain data may be our only reliable guide. However, in the depths of opacity, the high premium for freedom is always accompanied by unknown risks.

data.title: Visa Teams Up With BVNK to Enable Stablecoin Payouts Directly to Digital Wallets
data.description:
Visa Integrates Stablecoins Into Visa Direct With BVNK Partnership

Visa is partnering with BVNK to bring stablecoin payouts to its Visa Direct real-time payment network, blending blockchain settlement with one of the world’s largest traditional payment rails.

The integration will allow businesses in select markets to fund payouts using stablecoins and deliver funds directly to recipients’ digital wallets, bypassing traditional banking delays.

Exciting news: we're powering stablecoin payments for @Visa Direct

Starting this year with pilot programs, BVNK will provide stablecoin infrastructure for @VISADIRECT's $1.7 trillion real-time payments network, enabling faster, more flexible global money movement. pic.twitter.com/0SxgIRrhof

— BVNK (@BVNKFinance) January 14, 2026

How Will Stablecoins Change Payments

Under the partnership, BVNK, which processes over $30 billion in stablecoin transactions annually, will provide the infrastructure supporting Visa Direct’s stablecoin services.

Select business customers will be able to pre-fund payouts with stablecoins like USDC rather than relying solely on fiat currency balances.

The system enables recipients to receive “digital dollars” almost instantly, including on weekends, holidays, or when banks are closed.

#Visa has partnered with @BVNKFinance to enable stablecoin payouts through Visa Direct.

The integration lets select businesses pre fund payouts in stablecoins like $USDC and send funds directly to recipients’ digital wallets over @VISADIRECT, which moves about $1.7 trillion… pic.twitter.com/VCoHCePEbd

— Crypto Miners (@CryptoMiners_Co) January 14, 2026

This approach reduces delays caused by cut-off times, correspondent banks, and cross-border settlement friction.

Mark Nelsen, global head of product, commercial & money movement solutions at Visa, said,

“Stablecoins are an exciting opportunity for global payments, with enormous potential to reduce friction and expand access to faster, more efficient payment options - including during weekends, holidays and when banks are closed.

BVNK shares this vision, and that’s why we’re partnering with them to provide the reliable, trusted and necessary infrastructure needed to expand our Visa Direct stablecoin pilots.”

Why Visa Is Expanding Into Stablecoins

Visa’s move reflects growing demand for faster, always-on payment solutions, particularly for global business use cases.

Stablecoin payouts operate 24/7, offering speed and certainty for applications like gig economy payments, creator payouts, and cross-border payroll.

Corporate treasury teams also gain flexibility, choosing between fiat and on-chain settlement based on liquidity needs.

Visa Ventures previously invested in BVNK in May 2025, shortly after BVNK closed a $50 million Series B round.

This collaboration marks the next stage in Visa’s efforts to embed stablecoins directly into mainstream payments infrastructure rather than treating them as a parallel system.

Where Will This Rollout Take Place

The service will initially launch in markets with strong demand for digital asset payments, with broader global expansion planned over time.

The partnership gives businesses and consumers more choice in how and when funds move, bridging traditional payment networks with blockchain liquidity without replacing them.

Jesse Hemson-Struthers, CEO of BVNK, said,

“Visa and BVNK both believe in the transformational potential of stablecoin technology, not just as a payment method, but as a powerful layer of payments infrastructure.

This partnership is unlocking a new layer of payment innovation where stablecoins will be embedded directly into the world’s most trusted payments network.

It will give businesses and end consumers more choice in how and when they receive and send their funds.”

With Visa Direct processing an estimated $1.7 trillion in money movements annually, integrating stablecoins positions Visa as a bridge between traditional finance and blockchain-based value transfer, signalling that stablecoins are increasingly becoming a mainstream component of global payment infrastructure.

data.title: Bitchat Surges In Uganda After Government Blocks Off All Information Ahead Of Thursday Election
data.description:
Uganda’s Internet Shutdown Sparks Surge in Bitchat Usage Ahead of Election

Uganda has cut off nationwide internet access ahead of Thursday’s presidential election, citing concerns over online misinformation. Critics, however, argue the blackout is part of a pattern of information suppression that could influence electoral outcomes, marking the third consecutive election in which authorities have restricted connectivity.

⚠️ Confirmed: Live network data show a nation-scale disruption to internet connectivity in #Uganda; the measure comes days ahead of general elections and corresponds to a shutdown notice from the Uganda Communications Commission "to mitigate the rapid spread of misinformation" 📉 pic.twitter.com/01ZGYVRSuG

— NetBlocks (@netblocks) January 13, 2026

The shutdown, which took effect Tuesday at 6:00 pm local time, was confirmed by Uganda Communications Commission executive director Nyombi Thembo, who acknowledged the government has the technical ability to block Bitchat, an internet-free encrypted messaging app, but has not yet done so. Thembo’s earlier assurances that the internet would remain online have now proven false, raising questions about government transparency and intent.

🔥 JUST IN: Mesh network messaging app "Bitchat" adds ~1M users after Uganda opposition leader warns about nation-wide internet shutdown 😱 pic.twitter.com/5LVAiTD0SY

— Lightning News (@LightningNewsX) January 5, 2026

As the nation went dark, the people of Uganda has turned to Bitchat as an alternative, creating a mini-popularity boom and pushing the app to the top of the Apple App Store and Google Play charts in Uganda. Powered by Bluetooth mesh networks, the app enables users to send messages without internet connectivity, providing a vital channel for communication amid state-imposed restrictions.

The popularity of Bitchat in Uganda reflects a broader trend: when conventional online networks are curtailed, citizens turn to decentralized, offline messaging platforms. VPN apps also rank among the most downloaded in the country, highlighting Ugandans’ determination to stay connected despite the blackout.

Data from early January indicated that over 400,000 Ugandans had already downloaded Bitchat, a figure that has likely grown in the wake of the shutdown. For many users, the app has become an indispensable tool to share election news and maintain social connections during periods of restricted internet access.

A global solution to connectivity disruptions

Bitchat’s role in Uganda mirrors its usage in other countries experiencing internet restrictions or natural disasters. In September 2025, nearly 50,000 users in Nepal turned to Bitchat to bypass a temporary social media ban during corruption protests. Three weeks later, a similar surge occurred in Madagascar, and in November, Jamaicans used the app extensively when Hurricane Melissa disrupted conventional communications.

Last week, we observed a sudden spike in bitchat downloads from Indonesia during nationwide protests.

Today we're seeing an even bigger spike from Nepal during youth protests over government corruption and a social media ban.

Freedom tech is for the people. Please share. pic.twitter.com/IqhRa8eCvw

— calle (@callebtc) September 10, 2025

The app’s growing adoption underscores the increasing demand for resilient, internet-independent communication tools, especially in contexts where governments restrict digital access or traditional infrastructure is compromised.

As Uganda heads into a high-stakes election, Bitchat has emerged as a critical alternative, enabling citizens to maintain information flow even under the shadow of state-imposed internet blackouts.

data.title: Visa effect
data.description:
Author:Nishil JainArticle Compilation：Block unicorn

Foreword

In the 1960s, the credit card industry was in chaos. Banks across the United States were trying to build their own payment networks, but each network operated independently. If you held a Bank of America credit card, you could only use it at merchants that had agreements with Bank of America. And when banks tried to expand their business to other banks, all credit card payments encountered the problem of interbank settlement.

Implications for Stablecoins

In a sense, Anchorage Digital and other companies that now offer stablecoin as a service are replaying the BankAmericard story in the stablecoin space. They provide the underlying infrastructure for new issuers to build stablecoins, while liquidity is constantly being dispersed among new tokens.

Currently, there are over 300 stablecoins listed on the Defillama platform. Moreover, each newly created stablecoin is limited to its own ecosystem. Therefore, no single stablecoin can generate the network effect needed to go mainstream.

Since the same underlying assets support these new coins, why do we need more coins with new code? In our Visa story, these are like BankAmericards. Ethena, Anchorage Digital, M0, or Bridge—each allows a protocol to issue its own stablecoin, but this only exacerbates fragmentation in the industry. Ethena is another similar protocol that allows yield transfers and white-label customization of its stablecoin. Just like MegaETH issued USDm—they issued USDm through a tool that supports USDtb. However, this model failed. It only fragmented the ecosystem. In the credit card case, the brand differences between different banks are irrelevant because they don't cause any friction in the user-to-merchant payment process. The underlying issuance and payment layer is always Visa. However, this is not the case for stablecoins. Different token codes mean an unlimited number of liquidity pools. Merchants (or applications or protocols in this case) will not add all stablecoins issued by M0 or Bridge to their list of accepted stablecoins. They will decide whether to accept them based on their liquidity in the open market; the most held and most liquid coins should be accepted, and the rest will not. The Road Ahead: The Visa Model for Stablecoins [Image of a stablecoin] We need independent third-party institutions to manage stablecoins across different asset classes. Issuers and applications supporting these assets should be able to join cooperatives and earn reserve rewards. At the same time, they should also have governance rights, able to vote on the development direction of the stablecoins they choose. From a network effect perspective, this would be an excellent model. As more and more issuers and protocols join the same token, it will facilitate the widespread adoption of a token that keeps profits internally rather than flowing into other people's pockets.

data.title: Cryptocurrency trading: investment or gambling?
data.description:
In September 2025, the Journal of Financial and Quantitative Analysis published an article titled "Gambling on Crypto Tokens?". This paper used Google Trends as a proxy variable for retail investor interest and found that regions with a high gambling propensity showed stronger interest in both fungible and non-fungible (NFT) crypto tokens. The study indicated that this interest was significantly higher during crypto market bubbles and for lottery-like tokens. Furthermore, retail investor interest in cryptocurrencies declined after the legalization of sports betting. The study further found a positive correlation between increased token interest and increased participation and larger fundraising amounts. However, after periods of high interest, consumer credit default rates surged, and this phenomenon was limited to subprime borrowers. In summary, this research suggests that gambling preference is an important indicator for predicting retail investor interest in the crypto market.

I. Introduction

Since the advent of Bitcoin in 2009, the crypto market has experienced explosive growth. During this period, thousands of crypto tokens—digital assets created on blockchains (a decentralized, distributed digital ledger)—have emerged. These tokens can represent a variety of assets and uses: well-known examples such as Bitcoin and Ethereum are primarily used as a medium of exchange or a store of value, while other crypto tokens can be used to acquire specific products or services on the blockchain platform, or represent ownership of physical and digital items. Along with the market expansion, consumer interest has also grown, with over 20% of U.S. adults having invested in, traded, or used crypto tokens (CNBC (2022)), and an estimated 580 million crypto investors worldwide (Crypto.com (2024)).

Despite the significant growth in the retail crypto investor population, direct evidence regarding the characteristics of these investors is scarce due to the anonymity inherent in blockchain technology. Meanwhile, the surge in crypto investors has raised concerns among policymakers, particularly given the extreme volatility of the crypto market. For example, the total market capitalization of crypto reached nearly $2.8 trillion in November 2021, then plummeted to $1.2 trillion in June 2022 before rebounding to $2.6 trillion in May 2024 (Forbes (2024)). This dramatic volatility raises concerns that retail investors may not fully understand the associated risks. Specifically, crypto token returns exhibit a highly positive skewness, meaning there is a tiny probability of obtaining extremely high returns (Liu and Tsyvinski (2021), Liu, Tsyvinski, and Wu (2022)). This return pattern resembles lottery products and is highly attractive to investors with a strong gambling preference (Kumar (2009)). Therefore, this paper explores whether gambling preferences can predict retail investor interest in the crypto market. Understanding whether retail investors view crypto tokens as lottery-like products can help policymakers determine appropriate disclosure standards and regulatory frameworks (such as the legislative recommendations made by Lummis and Gillibrand (2023)). In the absence of direct data, this paper draws on the work of Da, Engelberg, and Gao (2011), using Google search interest as a proxy variable for retail investor interest, focusing on two significant types of crypto tokens: Initial Coin Offerings (ICOs) and the non-fungible token (NFT) series. Unlike tokens as general-purpose currencies, ICOs focus on project investment, while NFTs focus on digital ownership and collectibles. Consistent with the view that gambling preferences predict crypto interest, this paper finds significantly higher interest in crypto tokens in regions with higher per capita lottery sales. This finding is also robust to other gambling-related demographic characteristics identified by Kumar (2009) and Kumar, Page, and Spalt (2011). To alleviate concerns that "attention does not equal investment," this paper documents that attention to crypto wallets surged both before and after token issuance, with higher attention correlated with larger fundraising amounts and more participants. Furthermore, this paper excludes other explanatory paths such as advertising, risk appetite, or distrust of the system. This paper further explores the token hierarchy factors influencing gambling-driven attention. First, ICO and NFT projects launched during crypto market bubbles attract more attention in regions with high gambling propensity. Second, in the ICO market, tokens with lower opening prices (i.e., more "lottery-like" characteristics) and a lack of Know Your Customer (KYC) protocols (Li, Shin, and Wang (2021)) also generate greater interest in these regions. Furthermore, using the gradual legalization of sports betting in various US states as a natural experiment, this paper finds that attention to token issuance relatively decreased in regions with high gambling propensity after legal sports betting was permitted. This suggests that crypto tokens are largely seen as alternatives to traditional gambling products by retail investors. Finally, this paper examines the relationship between crypto attention and consumer credit consequences. Using data from Equifax, this paper finds that consumer credit default rates surged in regions with high gambling propensity following periods of high crypto attention, and this phenomenon was concentrated among financially constrained subprime borrowers. Lag analysis shows that the increase in attention preceded the rise in default rates. This paper contributes to several areas of literature: First, it provides new perspectives on the characteristics and motivations of retail investors in ICO market research (Li and Mann (2025), Lee and Parlour (2021), Cong, Li, and Wang (2021, 2022), etc.); Second, it enriches the NFT literature, revealing the relationship between retail investor attention and primary market performance (Kong and Lin (2021), Borri, Liu, and Tsyvinski (2022), Oh, Rosen, and Zhang (2023)); Third, it expands the literature on the impact of gambling preferences on the prices and trading volumes of financial products (Barberis and Huang (2008), Bali, Cakici, and Whitelaw (2011), Kumar (2009), Green and Hwang (2012)); Fourth, it connects research on retail investor behavior (Barber and Odean (2000, 2008), Welch (2022), Fedyk (2022), Barber et al. (2022)); Finally, this paper supplements emerging literature on the characteristics of crypto investors (Dhawan and Putniņš (2023), Hackethal et al. (2022), Kogan et al. (2024), Aiello et al. (2023), Divakaruni and Zimmerman (2024), Sun (2023)), demonstrating that gambling preference is an important factor in predicting retail investor interest in the crypto market. This section introduces the data sources used in the study and the descriptive statistics of the variables in the regression analysis. A. Data Sources 1. Retail Investor Attention This paper adopts the method of Da et al. (2011), using online attention obtained from Google Trends as a proxy variable for investment behavior. Its advantage lies in capturing investors' search intentions in a private environment. The study uses the Google Search Volume Index (SVI), with a value range of 0–100. This paper collects data at a more granular level of Specific Market Regions (DMAs), covering 209 DMAs in the United States. For each token project, the region with the highest attention (SVI=100) represents the project's relative popularity in that region. 2. Initial Coin Offering (ICO) An ICO is a way for blockchain startups to raise funds. Unlike an IPO, these tokens do not represent equity, but usually represent some kind of utility within the project's ecosystem. (1) Sample screening: The data is integrated from ICOBench.io, and projects that do not meet the soft cap and cannot be participated in by US investors are excluded. (2) Data volume: 937 completed ICOs from January 2016 to December 2018 are finally selected. (3) Contributor identification: Wallet addresses are obtained through white papers, and the number of unique wallet addresses is tracked using Etherscan.io to infer the actual number of contributors. 3. Non-fungible tokens (NFTs) NFTs represent ownership of unique items (such as works of art) on the blockchain. (1) Sample screening: The data comes from the largest trading platform, OpenSea. Since Google Trends often displays 0 for low transaction volume entries, this article focuses on the top 100 NFT series by transaction volume between 2017 and 2022. (2) Screening criteria: Items with more than 10,000 units or an average minting price of 0 were excluded. The final sample included 46 NFT series. 4. Regional demographic characteristics This paper uses per capita lottery sales as a proxy variable for regional gambling propensity. Data were manually collected from state gambling authorities and aggregated at the DMA level. To avoid "forward-looking bias", all demographic data were set at the 2015 baseline to capture static cross-sectional differences. 5. Consumer credit characteristics Default data were obtained from Equifax (overdue for more than 90 days is considered a default). This paper calculates the default rate in the DMA-year-month dimension and divides the population into subprime (<620) and non-subprime (≥620) groups based on credit scores for comparative study. B. Descriptive Statistics 1. Regional Characteristics: In 197 DMAs with lottery data, the average annual lottery expenditure per adult is $199, but this varies greatly by region (ranging from less than $1 to over $800). 2. ICO Characteristics: The average amount raised by an ICO is $26.3 million (approximately 40% of the hard cap). 36% of projects require KYC (Know Your Customer) verification, and 57% of projects have their code publicly available on GitHub. 3. NFT Characteristics: The median number of NFTs issued in the sample is approximately 9,200. The vast majority (approximately 90%) are active on Twitter and Discord, and 85% of projects advertise the inclusion of "rare items."

III. Regional Gambling Tendencies and Retail Investor Crypto Attention

This study examines how regional gambling tendencies affect the attention received by crypto tokens by estimating the following general regression model:

Where SVL represents the attention received by a specified market region (DMA) d for an ICO or NFT series i during the issuance period.

 IV. Factors Driving Gambling-Style Token Attention

In this section, we explore the factors that moderate retail investors' gambling-style attention to crypto tokens, including the characteristics of the tokens themselves and changes in the external gambling environment.

A. Token Characteristic Analysis

This paper examines specific token attributes that may induce gambling psychology in retail investors.
1. Low Price Characteristic (Lottery-like Attribute): According to existing literature (Kumar (2009)), low price is a core characteristic of lottery-like stocks. Empirical studies have found that ICO projects with lower opening prices on the first day receive significantly more attention from regions with high gambling propensity than high-priced projects.

Interaction coefficients show that low-priced tokens received an additional 5% increase in attention in these regions. 2. KYC Protocols and Risk Preference: Price manipulation tactics such as "pump and depressurize" (P&D) schemes are common in the crypto market, and these projects typically have weak KYC (Know Your Customer) vetting. Research found that ICOs lacking KYC protocols attract significantly more attention from retail investors in high-gambling-prone regions, suggesting that these investors are more inclined to participate in high-risk, unregulated projects. 3. Market Bubble/Boom Effect: This paper defines the period from the second half of 2017 to the beginning of 2018 as the "boom period" of the ICO market and the price surge in the NFT market between 2021 and 2022 as the "boom period." Regression results show that token projects launched during these two periods received significantly more attention from high-gambling-prone regions than during non-bubble periods. For NFTs, high-gambling-prone regions showed approximately 23% more attention to tokens during the bubble period than during the non-bubble period.

B. The Impact of Sports Gambling Legalization

To further confirm that crypto attention is driven by gambling preferences, this paper uses the phased legalization of sports gambling in various US states as a natural experiment. If crypto tokens are seen as alternatives to gambling, then attention to tokens should decrease when legal gambling channels emerge. This paper estimates the following regression model:

Where, PostSG is a dummy variable that takes a value of 1 when the state where DMA d is located has legalized sports gambling and the ICO occurs after the legalization date.

Main Conclusions:

1. Significant Substitution Effect: Empirical results show that after the legalization of sports betting, the attention paid to ICOs in relevant regions decreased significantly.
2. Stronger Response in Regions with High Gambling Propensity: After introducing the interaction term between "legalization of gambling" and "regional per capita lottery sales," a significant negative correlation was found. This indicates that in regions with a pre-existing high gambling propensity, the "crowding-out effect" of opening up sports betting on cryptocurrency attention is most pronounced.
3. Conclusion Summary: This finding strongly demonstrates that retail investors view cryptocurrency as a substitute for traditional gambling products. When residents have legal channels for sports betting to satisfy their gambling appetites, their attention to the cryptocurrency market decreases accordingly.

V. Retail Investor Crypto Attention and Consequences of Consumer Credit

Existing research (Barber and Odean (2000); Barber et al. (2022)) shows that retail investors often underperform in traditional stock markets. If they perform similarly poorly in the crypto market, they may fall into financial distress. Therefore, this section examines the association between retail investor crypto attention and subsequent consumer credit consequences, and investigates how this association changes with consumer credit constraints. This paper measures credit constraints using credit scores and divides them into subprime borrowers (score < 620) and non-subprime borrowers (≥ 620). Since the ICO sample is more comprehensive than the NFT sample, this section focuses on the relationship between retail investor attention to ICOs and consumer default rates.

Existing research (Barber and Odean (2000); Barber et al. (2022)) shows that retail investors often underperform in traditional stock markets. If they also underperform in the crypto market, they may fall into financial distress. Therefore, this section examines the association between retail investor crypto attention and subsequent consumer credit consequences, and investigates how this association changes with consumer credit constraints.

This paper estimates the following regression model across a specified market area (DMA) – credit segment – ​​year – month dimension: [Image of regression model] The dependent variable is the change in default rate between the current month t and the next 6 months (t+6). HighSVI is a dummy variable representing the top third of monthly attention. Key Conclusions: 1. Association between Crypto Craze and Default Rate: The study found a significantly positive interaction term between per capita lottery sales (gambling tendency) and ICO attention indicators. This indicates that in regions with high game-theoretic tendencies and high ICO attention, subsequent consumer credit default rates show a significant increase. 2. Vulnerability of Subprime Borrowers: Further analysis shows that the surge in default rates was entirely driven by subprime borrowers. In regions with both high game-theoretic tendencies and high attention, the default rate for subprime borrowers increased by an average of approximately 2.3% over six months. In contrast, the default situation for non-subprime borrowers (those with better financial conditions) did not change significantly. 3. Lead-Lag Relationship and Preceding Trend Test: To rule out the possibility that default behavior itself caused the increase in attention, this paper conducted a preceding trend analysis on the changes in default rates. The conclusion shows that in the period before the surge in attention (t-6 to t), there were no significant differences in default rates across regions (no preceding trend); in the period after the surge in attention (t+1 to t+6), the subprime default rate in regions with high game-theoretic tendencies began to climb significantly. This lead-lag relationship suggests that the hype surrounding the crypto market foreshadowed the subsequent credit deterioration, not the other way around. **Summary:** This chapter demonstrates that game-driven crypto asset investment tendencies have negative economic consequences for vulnerable financial groups. For subprime borrowers already facing financial constraints, participation in such high-risk, lottery-like crypto investments often carries the risk of subsequent real financial default. **VI. Conclusion** This paper delves into the fundamental drivers of retail investor participation in the cryptocurrency market, finding that gambling preferences are the core factor explaining this phenomenon. By analyzing Google Trends search data, this paper confirms that in regions with higher per capita lottery sales and a stronger speculative atmosphere, retail investors show significantly higher interest in Initial Coin Offerings (ICOs) and Non-Fungible Tokens (NFTs) projects compared to other regions. This interest is not inflated; it is highly synchronized with the download and use of crypto wallets and directly and positively impacts the amount of funding raised and the number of participants in the primary market. Further moderating effect analysis shows that this gambling-driven investment motive is particularly strong during market "bubbles" and when tokens possess "lottery-like characteristics" (such as extremely low unit prices, lack of identity verification protocols/KYC, and susceptibility to price manipulation). The study also found, through a natural experiment using the legalization of sports betting in various US states, that when legal betting channels appear, the previously active interest in crypto tokens significantly decreases, strongly demonstrating that retail investors view crypto tokens as alternatives to traditional gambling products. Most importantly, this speculative behavior based on gambling preferences poses a substantial threat to individual and societal financial health. Using micro-data from Equifax Credit Bureau, the study found that a surge in crypto interest in regions with high gambling propensity often foreshadows a rise in consumer default rates in the following months, and this credit deterioration is concentrated entirely among the subprime borrowers with the weakest financial resilience. This finding shatters the simplistic illusion that "crypto assets are inclusive financial instruments," revealing their potential predatory nature as speculative tools that plunder the wealth of the lower classes. In conclusion, this paper provides important academic evidence for global regulators: crypto assets are largely used by retail investors as a new form of gambling; therefore, regulation of these assets should not be limited to financial risks but should also consider public health and consumer protection, establishing stricter disclosure standards and entry thresholds.

data.title: Pakistan Taps Trump-Linked World Liberty Financial to Test Stablecoin Payments
data.description:
Pakistan Tests Stablecoins as Part of State-Backed Payments Push

Pakistan has just signed an agreement to explore the use of a dollar-pegged stablecoin, marking the country's growing interest in blockchain-based settlement systems.

According to a Reuters report, the agreement involves SC Financial Technologies, a sister company of World Liberty Financial, the crypto venture associated with the family of U.S. President Donald Trump.

Today, World Liberty Financial signed an MoU with the Ministry of Finance to explore innovation in digital finance, particularly the use of stablecoins for cross-border transactions, signalling growing global interest in Pakistan as a key market for digital assets. pic.twitter.com/rYzbfHYysd

— Pakistan Virtual Assets Regulatory Authority (@PakistanVARA) January 14, 2026

The partnership would give Pakistan assess of World Liberty’s USD1 stablecoin which the country would utilise for cross-border transactions, marking one of the earliest known cases of a sovereign state formally evaluating a privately issued stablecoin for payments.

Under the reported arrangement, World Liberty Financial is expected to work with the State Bank of Pakistan to integrate USD1 into the country’s emerging digital payments architecture. The stablecoin would operate alongside existing systems, potentially supporting regulated use cases such as remittances, corporate settlements, and international transfers.

While the full terms of the agreement have not been disclosed, Pakistan is expected to formally announce the partnership during a visit to Islamabad by World Liberty chief executive Zach Witkoff. Public information on SC Financial Technologies remains limited, underscoring the early-stage and exploratory nature of the initiative.

The move reflects a broader shift among governments toward testing blockchain-based payment instruments not as speculative assets, but as infrastructure components capable of reducing settlement times and costs in cross-border finance.

USD1 adoption highlights institutional stablecoin momentum

USD1, the dollar-pegged stablecoin issued by World Liberty Financial, currently carries a market capitalization of approximately $3.4 billion, according to CoinMarketCap data. The token has already been used in large-scale transactions, including a reported $2 billion equity deal involving Binance and Abu Dhabi–based state investment firm MGX.

Pakistan Explores USD1 Stablecoin for Cross-Border Payments

Pakistan is reportedly exploring the use of USD1, a dollar-backed stablecoin issued by World Liberty Financial, a crypto venture linked to the Trump family, for cross-border payments.

The stablecoin could be integrated… pic.twitter.com/wexwugarG1

— ME Group (@MetaEraHK) January 14, 2026

Earlier this week, World Liberty expanded its ecosystem with the launch of World Liberty Markets, an onchain lending and borrowing platform built around USD1 and its WLFI governance token. The platform allows users to post crypto assets such as Ether, tokenized Bitcoin, and other stablecoins as collateral, reinforcing the project’s focus on institutional and capital markets use cases.

Pakistan positions itself as a testing ground for digital finance

Pakistan’s engagement with stablecoins fits into a broader national push to modernize its financial infrastructure and position itself as a regional crypto hub. Over the past year, authorities have moved to formalize the sector by establishing a dedicated virtual assets regulator, allowing major exchanges to operate domestically, and exploring initiatives such as Bitcoin reserves and real-world asset tokenization.

About yesterday at binance meetup event in islamabad,Pakistan binance is doing such a wonderful work to spread awarness of crypto in Pakistan looking forward to have it in my home city chakwal,Pakistan lets keep building guys @binance@cz_binance@UmairNauman_pic.twitter.com/k1AJPcvc7R

— Mohammadrafay (@Mohamma95814515) January 12, 2026

Officials have increasingly framed blockchain technology as a tool to address inefficiencies in cross-border payments and remittances, a critical component of Pakistan’s economy. Against this backdrop, testing a regulated stablecoin model offers policymakers a way to experiment with digital settlement rails without committing to a full central bank digital currency.

If implemented, the USD1 pilot could provide insight into how privately issued stablecoins might coexist with state oversight, offering a glimpse into how emerging markets are shaping the next phase of digital payments infrastructure.
